name: Ix Tab
description: Ix Tab, also known as Ixtab, is the Mayan goddess of suicide. She is primarily associated with suicide by hanging, which was considered a noble and honorable death in Mayan culture, guaranteeing passage to paradise. Unlike the negative connotations often associated with suicide in other cultures, Ix Tab was viewed as a guide and protector for those who chose this path. She is often depicted with a rope around her neck, signifying the act of suicide, and with closed eyes, indicating death. Some accounts portray her as benevolent, escorting souls to the afterlife, while others suggest a more ambivalent role. Her presence highlights the Mayan understanding of death as a transition rather than an ending, and the acceptance of suicide under specific circumstances, such as sacrifice or escape from suffering. Ix Tab's role reflects the complex Mayan worldview, where death could be both tragic and honorable, and where deities played an active role in guiding souls through the afterlife. She embodies not only the act of self-destruction but also the promise of a better existence beyond the earthly realm. It is important to note that the Mayan view on suicide was nuanced and not universally accepted, with societal factors influencing its perception. Ix Tab's existence within the Mayan pantheon provides a unique insight into their spiritual beliefs and the acceptance of death as a part of life's cycle. Her influence is primarily seen in depictions of death and funerary practices, reflecting the belief in her role as a psychopomp for those who died by suicide.
subtitle: Mayan Goddess of Suicide by Hanging

keyMyths.title: The Rope's Embrace
keyMyths.description: Ix Tab is said to guide those who die by hanging to a special paradise. This act was considered honorable in certain Mayan contexts, ensuring a favorable afterlife.

keyMyths.title: The Guide to Paradise
keyMyths.description: Unlike other death deities who might be feared, Ix Tab is sometimes seen as a benevolent guide. She escorts souls to a better place after their earthly suffering.
keyMyths.source: Scholarly interpretations of Mayan funerary practices

keyMyths.title: Ambivalent Protector
keyMyths.description: While often seen as a guide, some interpretations suggest Ix Tab's role is more complex. She may not always offer salvation, reflecting the nuanced view of suicide in Mayan society.
keyMyths.source: Analysis of Mayan art and religious texts

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While specific details are scarce, it's likely offerings were made to Ix Tab to ensure a peaceful transition in the afterlife for those who died by suicide. These offerings may have included food, drink, or valuable items.

worshipPractices.name: Rituals of Passage
worshipPractices.description: Rituals surrounding suicide were likely performed, perhaps involving prayers or ceremonies to honor Ix Tab and ensure the deceased's acceptance into paradise. The details of these rituals remain largely unknown due to the loss of Mayan texts.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ix Tab's legacy is complex. She challenges Western perspectives on suicide, prompting discussions about cultural differences in understanding death and the afterlife. She appears in modern fiction and art, often as a symbol of both despair and hope, and as a reminder of the Mayan civilization. Her image continues to spark interest in Mayan mythology.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Ix Tab are somewhat obscure within Mayan mythology, as detailed creation myths focus more on the principal creator gods and the origins of humanity. Unlike many deities with elaborate birth stories, Ix Tab's emergence seems tied directly to the act of suicide itself, suggesting she may have arisen as a personification of this specific type of death and its perceived honor. Given the societal acceptance of suicide in certain circumstances, it is plausible that Ix Tab was not seen as a malevolent or frightening figure, but rather as a compassionate guide for those who chose to end their lives. Her existence highlights the Mayan understanding of death as a transition rather than an end, and her role ensures safe passage to the afterlife. The lack of detailed origin stories underscores her function as a facilitator of this transition.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Specific myths and stories directly involving Ix Tab are scarce, largely due to the destruction of Mayan codices and the loss of oral traditions. However, her role is understood through her iconography and association with suicide by hanging. She is often depicted with a rope around her neck, signifying the manner of death she oversees. Instead of active narratives, Ix Tab's presence is more symbolic, representing the acceptance and even honor associated with suicide in certain contexts, such as sacrifice or escaping dishonor. She is believed to escort the souls of those who died by hanging to paradise, a realm of peace and abundance. The absence of elaborate myths suggests her importance lies more in her function as a spiritual guide than in heroic deeds or divine conflicts. Her story is less about personal adventures and more about her sacred duty.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct evidence of a specific cult dedicated solely to Ix Tab is limited. However, her association with suicide suggests that certain rituals and beliefs were likely associated with her. It is plausible that Mayan priests or shamans played a role in preparing individuals for suicide, perhaps invoking Ix Tab to ensure a peaceful transition. Offerings may have been made to appease her and secure her favor for the deceased. The lack of extensive archaeological evidence makes it difficult to reconstruct specific worship practices. However, given the Mayan emphasis on the afterlife and the importance of proper burial rites, it is reasonable to assume that Ix Tab was recognized and honored in some way. Her presence serves as a reminder of the Mayan worldview, where death was not necessarily feared but seen as a natural part of the cosmic cycle.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ix Tab's legacy extends beyond ancient Mayan civilization. She offers a unique perspective on suicide, contrasting with the often negative views prevalent in Western cultures. Her image challenges contemporary understandings of death and the afterlife, prompting reflections on cultural biases and the diverse ways societies perceive these concepts. In modern art and literature, Ix Tab appears as a symbol of both despair and hope, representing the complex emotions associated with suicide. She serves as a reminder of the rich and complex Mayan civilization and their sophisticated understanding of the human condition. Her presence in popular culture sparks interest in Mayan mythology and encourages a deeper exploration of their beliefs and practices. Her legacy endures as a testament to the enduring power of mythology to challenge and inform our understanding of the world.

powers: Guidance of souls to the afterlife
shortDescription: Ix Tab is the Mayan goddess of suicide, particularly suicide by hanging. She guides and protects those who choose this path, leading them to paradise.
